title: Finding the Square Root of a Perfect Square

steps.work: We need a number that, when multiplied by itself, gives 81.
steps.explanation: The square root is the inverse of squaring. If n^2 = 81, then sqrt(81) = n.
Knowing the first 15 or so perfect squares helps: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144.

title: Estimating the Square Root of a Non-Perfect Square

steps.work: 4^2 = 16 and 5^2 = 25. Since 16 < 20 < 25, sqrt(20) is between 4 and 5.
steps.explanation: Bracket the number between two perfect squares to narrow down the range.

steps.work: 20 is 4 units above 16 and 5 units below 25. It is slightly closer to 16, so sqrt(20) is slightly closer to 4 — around 4.4 or 4.5.
steps.explanation: The proportion (4 out of 9 units from 16 to 25) gives a rough estimate of 4 + 4/9 ≈ 4.4.

steps.work: 4.5^2 = 20.25. That is very close to 20! Try 4.4: 4.4^2 = 19.36. So sqrt(20) is between 4.4 and 4.5, closer to 4.5.
steps.explanation: We can estimate sqrt(20) ≈ 4.5. The actual value is about 4.472.
